title: What Has Changed in Thirty Days of Tapping for Sleep
subtitle: A closing journal practice for marking what is different and making the changes conscious
excerpt: A journal practice for Day 30 of a 30-day sleep programme. The prompts move through the arc of the journey, what sleep looked like on Day 1, what it looks like now, what the body has learned, and what the next chapter holds. The aim is to make the change legible so it holds.
sourceNotes: Adapted from the Day 30 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795).

content.text: A closing journal practice for Day 30 of a 30-day sleep programme. Day 30 theme: claim rest as a birthright. The prompts move through the full arc, what the relationship with sleep looked like at the start, what it looks like now, what the body has learned to do differently. Writing it down makes the change visible and harder to dismiss on difficult nights.
Describe your relationship with sleep on Day 1. What were the patterns, the fears, the habits? What did a bad night feel like, and how often did one happen?
What were you hoping this programme would change? Write the specific things, the hours, the feelings on waking, the dread before bed, the mornings.

Write what is different now. Be specific: body sensations at bedtime, thoughts when you wake in the night, how mornings feel, what the relationship with the body around sleep feels like. Include small shifts as well as large ones.
What does the body know now that it didn't know on Day 1? What signal has been trained in, about safety, stillness, what night means?
What do I want to carry forward?
Which practices, which moments, which reframes do you want to bring with you? Write the specific things that held, the ones worth keeping as a permanent part of how you rest.
Adapted from the Day 30 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
